sentences.text: They started in our city-- in some other places maybe sooner-- in our city, it started in June, 1942.
That was the first very massive deportation.
And my mother's [SIGHS] two sisters and grandfather went.
And one sister still stayed.
Now my aunt's husband, as they were going, they ask him to run.
And he couldn't run too fast, because he was carrying a lot of clothes, everything on his back.
And so they shot him.
And the end, who didn't know who else went, said to one man from these ordinances, the Jewish police, said, tell-- tell my family that-- because she knew that he knew us-- that I am a widow.
And she-- she went.
And the streets were flowing with blood because a lot of people were killed as they were being taken.
And it was absolutely dreadful.
But we still believed that the-- they-- some of them who were not killed on the streets, that they will survive.
And we didn't know that they went to [NON-ENGLISH].
Some may be in Treblinka.
Well, at any rate, they were killed.
Well, you see, they said in ghetto before that you should have some employment which is recognized.
Now, my mother was working in a factory called Optima, in the office.
And she said, since you know how to sew, I suggest that you go over there.
And since I learned in school also how to embroider, I was making the buttonholes on the uniforms.
And so I had a job.
And I got so called [NON-ENGLISH], with a stamp that I could stay.
Now, my mother's sister believed that, oh, maybe it's just a hoax, and whatever.
And that's why they-- they-- whoever didn't have this thing went.
Later on, they were taking just anybody.
But the first one, people who had those [NON-ENGLISH] with the stamp stayed.
And so that's how I stayed.
And my mother's sister, who worked in another place called, Malrich, would later on, took over this Optima also.
And she later on went with another transport [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] accidentally.
She didn't have her papers with her.
She put them someplace.
And so she had to go.
And that was the one in which also my husband's mother went.
Her-- his-- her-- his brother went with the very first transport.
No.
We were-- all know each other.
But after the second, the October Aussiedlung.
In the meantime, we had so-called [NON-ENGLISH].
And we were about to go.
But after the Aussiedlung, my husband, he going to tell you how he survived this one, because he was also about to go with his mother.
Well, at any rate, we decided that we will get married.
And so I wouldn't be alone, and my mother was supposed to be different place, not together.
And then I got typhoid fever.
And my husband got it.
And when we recovered, it was just time to go to a concentration camp.
And once we were over there, we still had the papers, and we were coming to the factory.
And we had those papers.
[SIGHS] Somebody else run away, and they decimated the whole place where he worked.
And my mother said, we cannot do that.
Because we cannot save our life and-- with the knowledge that somebody else was killed because of us.
So we stayed.
Killed.
Before, when somebody run away from the ghetto, it was nothing.
It's simply somebody disappeared.
They could have said that somebody died.
But once you were-- you see in concentration already, they were keeping already the-- the figures and everything.
So you-- at that time--
When you go-- went to concentration camp and were working in certain place.
They knew exactly how many people in which place.
One guy from our place run away.
And that was nice of them.
They killed only his best friend.
They called us on so-called appel.
Well, standing was very hard that day.
I remember I nearly fainted.
Later on, I learned that in so-called [NON-ENGLISH].
Because we were standing maybe seven hours in big sun.
And very slowly, the Germans were going around.
And finally, they called his friend, the friend of the guy who run away, although he didn't know anything, and they shot him.
Yes.
My mother's friend knew a Polish fellow who are doing such papers for money.
But he was a terrific guy.
He would have done it, probably, even for us free.
And she introduced us.
And first he had to find someone, let's say, who died.
So he found out a girl my age who died.
And my name change to-- she was actually a countess.
Liliana [NON-ENGLISH].
My mother had other papers, and Max had other papers.
And we were supposed to run away and stay in another city.
I was a little-- now you see, before that, I could have saved myself.
Because the priest in our school came to my mother, and he said-- he was a secretary to the Archbishop over there.
And he said, I could put Erna in a convent.
But I was afraid, because there was one Jewish girl, 18 years old, very gorgeous, who was going from convent to convent, and when she discovered the Jewish child-- and the kid was killed.
Now, the best part was that her younger sister, when I was about eight, we went to camp together.
And she came to visit her sister.
So although I have changed since then, she would have recognized me.
Now, my mother had a cousin who married a gentile.
He was the best surgeon in the city.
And they had two very small children.
One was a year and a half, and the other was born just before the war.
And they were in a convent.
Because they were so-called mischling.
Father was a gentile, but according to Hitler, to the third generation, you are Jewish.
And she discovered them.
However, Professor Pietkofski, that was his name, went to the Germans.
At that time, they were bringing Germans from a Russian front, and he was saving many lives.
And he said, if you're going to take my children, you operate on your people.
I'm not going to do it.
So they released him.
But the fact was that she recognized somehow, although she never thought that they were Jewish children.
So my mother said, no because it's dangerous.
Yes.
Her name was Stephanie Branchstetter.
She survived, came to United States.
And of course, my friends recognized her on the street.
And she had papers from the German.
And she had a Swiss woman who was swearing she was born in Switzerland, and that she was German, which she was.
We couldn't prove it.
And so I don't know where she is now, but she survived and was-- she was living in an Essex house.
Married a multimillionaire, and was absolutely gorgeous.
But she was-- [SIGHS] she has at least 300 Jewish children on her conscience.
Yes.
Well, in the last month, that happened exactly on the 13th of March, 1943.
And again, you have to work in certain place that they would approve you going over there.
So they made two ghettos.
They made Ghetto A and Ghetto B.
And Ghetto B were older people and children.
We knew it's going to be bad.
Now, I, at that time, was already married.
I was married since November 15, 1942.
And this was already March.
I managed-- my husband asked-- he was very well liked by the Germans.
He was blonde and looked a little bit German.
They identify him with their own brothers and whatever.
He was very well liked.
He was just lucky this way.
And he managed to get me on the place where he was working.
And we were going out.
My mother went much earlier with her place.
And we're standing.
And I saw the whole carnage.
I saw-- there was a woman, very good friend of ours, and she had a little girl, four-years-old.
Her husband went to concentration camp, and she was supposed to go.
But she saw what-- the girl was so beautiful and so sweet.
And she couldn't leave the child.
And I saw her.
The child was dead.
And she was laying, dying on-- on the street.
And she told me, tell my husband we're dead.
And all those dead thing.
I-- this was a nightmare.
I-- sometimes I have dreams, especially when I read in the paper about the anti-Semitic feeling, and everything.
I see the face of this girl and this lady, and there was another one and another one.
And we are going among those-- those people shot.
And it-- it was unbelievable.
It-- it really was.
People who went early didn't see that.
They just left one place of work after an another.
But we were the one who later on were cleaning also that time.
And then some people were still hidden.
Now, my husband managed-- oh, he going to tell you this probably, most likely.
He was putting them in a big place and was getting them out of ghetto if he found them, but otherwise the Germans were shooting them.
And whichever room we went, when we were cleaning ghetto, it was blood all over the place.
It-- it was so-- you know, I saw one German taking a child, and taking him by his legs, and ripping him up to here and throwing against the wall.
They were all fathers and-- and-- and brothers and everything.
And to do such thing!
It's incomprehend.
If I wouldn't see it, I wouldn't believe it.
And it was so bad that I never saw a movie or anything which would, in-- in the brutality of this thing, which would be similar to-- to what was going on.
It was unbelievable.
And I believe that even the pogroms which happened you know, on Ukraine, and over, could not have been as bad as what this was there.
Country of-- of Goethe, of Schiller, of Beethoven, and all the great Germans, and that's how they behaved.
It-- it was-- they actually were persuaded that we were [NON-ENGLISH], and they were the supermen.
However, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] now, here, people are protesting if you killed little animal and make a fur coat.
But still, killing people, it's still going on, the terrorism and everything.
But this was on a very, very large scale.
Remember that Cracow, before the war, had about 70,000 inhabitants.
And out of those, I don't know whether they were really true Krakovian people because there were also people from other cities brought in.
Maybe 5,000 survive, which was a very small, very small number.
About 1,000 because of Schindler, and the rest from all over.
So it was extremely small number.
But this-- this was-- I am one of the very few people who are still alive who have seen this.
No, I was not in Ghetto B.
I was--
--with Ghetto A.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
No.
It was, in Polish, it's Ghetto A, and Ghetto B.
That-- that's--
That was that day.
[INAUDIBLE] I was coming quite late on March 13, 1943.
And my mother.
Not my mother, because my mother went much earlier with her place of work.
So when they were living there, wholesale killing didn't start yet.
Occasionally they maybe shot someone.
But she didn't see this, only my husband and I.
Well, of all the people who went with our place.
There were about 300 people.
And all of them, of course, have seen it.
Some are still alive.
Oh, yes.
As a matter of fact, in 1967, they caught two people, mind you, and I testified.
That was extension of the Nuremberg Trials, and were held in Kiel, in Germany.
That's Schleswig-Holstein.
And my husband and I and very many other people were asked to come.
Some couldn't go to testify, because they said it's two week experience.
I went, although they wrote me letters that I am going to be killed if I come.
So they were putting us in special hotel.
And wherever I went, on my left side was a wife of their mayor of that city, Burgermeister.
On the other side, wife of a bishop.
Were always flanked by those two, besides police, and so on.
I testified.
And in the newspapers, which I have someplace in the attic.
I try to find it.
Eventually, maybe someday I'll find.
They said that I put the last nail in the coffin.
Each one of the guy got 99 years.
And my husband came.
And he knew them better than I did.
And they were nice to him.
And he couldn't testify.
He-- he got sick, actually.
And although at that time he was still OK.
But it was a terrible experience seeing them over there, sitting.
And there were not just one judge, but just whole panel of judges.
And it-- after I came out from the-- one guy start running towards me.
Because there were children in-- in the court.
Although this was during vacation.
And he start running.
So police right away went around.
And he said, I just want to shake her hand.
Because until now, I didn't believe this thing.
But I believed in this testimony.
And he said, I want to shake her hand and apologize.
It was nice, nice fellow.
And that was it.
What other atrocities do you remember that you observed?
At that time.
At that time.
Well, the shooting, the killing, and those two children who lived with us, [?
Ruthie ?] and [?
Volfie. ?] I was later on sorting the clothes.
And I found their coats.
And they were shot, both of them, straight through the heart.
I-- when I found this later, as I was sorting the clothes, I simply couldn't take it.
But this shooting all around, and we had to step even over some dead bodies.
It was incredible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said that during the first deportation, there was blood flowing on the street, that they have to hose this down.
This time, it was much-- I mean, the whole world was-- was red.
It's-- the picture was absolutely incredible.
And that was actually the first time that I said to myself, I don't think we will survive.
Because it's going to be more and more of this thing.
And we were hoping that maybe the war will end soon.
But it didn't.
Well, I was one of the lucky few.
Because I was coming out every day to clean the ghetto from concentration camp.
And then somehow, we were able to see some of our Catholic gentile friends who were bringing us some food.
And we put a lot of money, a lot of jewelry, to my husband's former worker, who was also took over when his father died before the war.
And he was coming also in the beginning and bringing us some food.
And so it wasn't-- And my mother was working for this guy [?
Mudrich ?], who every day was giving every worker around bread.
So she was bringing it.
So we-- as we're coming also to the barracks, we could have the bread.
And my mother was bringing this for my husband's cousins.
And I was able to bring some other kind of food, some butter, and even some fruit and vegetable.
Of course, once they caught us, and they were about to kill us.
I ate half a pound of butter, because if they found it, I knew.
They were beating you up to a bloody pulp.
So I said, they not going to find anything from me, even if I have to drop dead to do it eating this, which I ate.
I had to.
But at any rate, they took us, and they gave each one of us 25.
They were about to kill us.
But the guy for whom we work, his name was Bulb.
He came in, and he said, release my people.
So just before they were about to shoot the whole place.
Of course they-- once in a while, they were looking what you are bringing into camp, and especially if you are bringing some food, then they were killing you.
So he came in and saw in the last second, they took us.
However, what I have seen, at that day, I will also never forget.
There was a place where only 40 people work. 39 men and 1 woman.
She was absolutely gorgeous.
She was married to the engineer who was in charge.
They called it, SS Bonarka, because the place in Cracow where they worked, they were doing some-- I don't know exactly.
But for the Germans, something, building whatever.
And they also brought a lot of food.
They were just before us coming in.
And they took them on the little hill where they were killing.
And they took us.
We're supposed to be killed next.
And the Ukrainians were killing those people.
And after they shot all of them, they shot the girl, but such a way that she was not dead yet.
And then they raped her.
And when I saw it, I said, oh my God, why don't I have-- my mother asked me to carry poison with me, and I didn't.
Because I said, that's what they're going to do also to me.
And she took her hand and put on her husband, who was already dead.
And I saw it.
And this is another picture which I carry with me, and I cannot forget.
She still had two sisters.
Both of them survived.
Very beautiful girl.
One, I think, married some English soldier after the war, and she moved to England.
But she-- of course, I never told her what I have seen.
None of us told her.
Because we knew that it would-- her mother was still alive also, that would devastate them.
So I kept it in.
And it's-- it was-- it was-- it still is a nightmare.
Her name was-- I don't remember her first name, but her name was, married name, Schpringood.
And I knew this family very well because my mother's brother, my uncle, when he was an apprentice, he was working for this guy, this engineer, father.
He taught him a lot.
Later on, he went to Argentina.
And he was in Buenos Aires and over there, and also in Paris, he learned his trade.
But he was his first guy.
So I knew the family very well.
And this young man, he was a very fine engineer.
He was in his 20s, and she was maybe 21 years old, and very, very beautiful girl.
And dying like this, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If I would have been freed, at that time, I would have gone on a killing binge.
I would have been killing all the Germans because it was-- I was so bitter and so frustrated that you have no idea.
It took me quite a while after the war to-- to become-- to-- to change into a human being.
Because I was completely different.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
Well, now I have to tell you this story.
When we are still-- because later on, once we liquidate ghetto, we worked in some magazines where we were sorting those things and packing them and sending to Germany.
It was wintertime.
And they set fires on the street.
And we were taking all the pots and pans from all the apartments, bringing over there.
And they were being washed.
Now, I got some sort of hepatitis at that time.
And I was work a little slower.
And there was a man, who before the war, he knew me.
Because he was selling fish on the Jewish fish place.
And my mother said to me, you never talk to strangers.
And I was coming with our housekeeper to buy the fish.
And he said to me-- I will remember this-- how are you, little girl?
And I didn't answer him, because mother said, don't talk.
He remembered this.
And he saw me walking slower, and got behind me.
And he kicked me in the spine so that I had fallen down.
And he started beating me and kicking me.
And then, of course, the German who actually liked me, his name was Unterscharfuhrer Adolbert Tzuchsberger.
They didn't catch him after the war.
He lost half of his hand on the Russian front.
That's why they send him to a nice job.
And he saw him hitting me.
Although he always was nice to me, he came in, and he kicked me so, and smashed my face, and my nose.
He broke my nose.
That I was half dead.
In order to get back to a concentration camp, luckily, they asked us to work by 5:00, and we had to lock our hands, so we wouldn't run away.
We couldn't just walk like this.
So two girls got me, and held me, dragged me.
That's when my mother saw me.
She took me to this [NON-ENGLISH], where most of the doctors, she knew from before the war.
One guy said, OK.
You stay in [NON-ENGLISH].
I said, I won't.
I'm going to work tomorrow.
He said, how could you walk?
I will walk.
I got so upset.
If a German would hit me, I would not get upset, because it was a German.
But a Jew, this was-- so my mother went to the men's barrack, which was not permitted, women to go there.
And she was sitting there, and he said his name was Jhan.
We called him Jash Feldman.
And she said, Jash, I lost my whole family.
She already knew.
I don't have a father, husband, brother, sister, cousin, whatever.
And this is all I have, my daughter.
Why are you hitting her?
He said, you think you are a high life-- he actually used this word, high life-- before the war?
Now you are nothing.
